description: Manually-curated experiment and literature proposals. This file is read by build_experiment_indexes.py and merged into experiment_proposals.v1.json. Items here survive pipeline regeneration. Add new items here; do NOT add them directly to experiment_proposals.v1.json.

items.objective: Test whether committed operating mode produces measurably lower harm outcomes than ablated (uncommitted) mode, and whether this gap narrows in volatile environments (gate-ablation design).
items.design: 2x2: condition A = committed BG gate active, condition B = gate ablated (beta suppressed), crossed with stable vs volatile environment. 2 matched seeds. PASS: harm_committed < harm_ablated in stable env; gap narrows or reverses in volatile env.
items.notes: Depends on ARC-016 (provisional) and MECH-090 (active). This is the behavioral consequence layer split from ARC-016 into ARC-029 during 2026-03-22 governance session.

items.objective: Ground ARC-029 in habitual vs goal-directed neuroscience: dorsolateral vs dorsomedial striatum dissociation, beta persistence during habitual sequences, model-free/model-based dual-process accounts.
key_sources: Balleine & O'Doherty (2010) - Human and rodent homologues in action control: corticostriatal determinants of goal-directed and habitual action
DLS/DMS lesion studies distinguishing outcome-sensitive vs stimulus-response behavior
Beta oscillation persistence during committed action sequences (Khanna & Carmena 2015 or similar)
Daw et al. (2005) - Uncertainty-based competition between prefrontal and dorsolateral striatal systems for behavioral control

items.objective: Ground ARC-029 in hierarchical RL and options frameworks: option-critic architecture, Feudal Networks, two-step task model-based switching.
key_sources: Bacon, Harb & Precup (2017) - The Option-Critic Architecture
Vezhnevets et al. (2017) - FeUdal Networks for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
Daw, Gershman et al. (2011) - Model-based influences on humans choices and striatal prediction errors (two-step task)

items.objective: Ground MECH-072 (foreseeable-harm gating reduces false attribution) in causal attribution neuroscience: mirror neuron agency detection, Bayesian causal inference, TPJ role in intentional attribution, cerebellar efference copy in self/other discrimination.
key_sources: Gallese et al. (1996) / Rizzolatti & Craighero (2004) - Mirror neuron system and action understanding
Kording & Wolpert (2004) - Bayesian integration in sensorimotor learning (causal inference framework)
Decety & Lamm (2007) - The role of the right temporoparietal junction in social interaction
Wolpert, Ghahramani & Jordan (1995) - An internal model for sensorimotor integration (efference copy)

items.objective: Ground MECH-072 in ML causal attribution and world model separation: Dreamer-style world model, causal representation learning, curiosity-driven exploration.
key_sources: Hafner et al. (2020) - Dream to Control: Learning Behaviors by Latent Imagination (Dreamer)
Scholkopf et al. (2021) - Toward Causal Representation Learning
Pathak et al. (2017) - Curiosity-driven Exploration by Self-supervised Prediction (self/world separation in ICM)

items.objective: Validate Route-2 MECH-104 wiring: unexpected harm events should spike commitment uncertainty (beta variance) via surprise gate, distinct from the jitter noise floor baseline.
items.design: Condition A = harm-surprise-gated beta variance (unexpected harm spike triggers variance increase). Condition B = jitter-only control (no harm-triggered spike). PASS: A shows significantly higher beta_variance on unexpected harm events than B; B shows stable low-level jitter only.
items.notes: EXQ-049e PASS validated Route-1 (noise floor preventing variance collapse). This experiment validates Route-2 (harm-surprise triggering volatility spike). Both routes needed for full MECH-104 validation.

items.objective: Deepen biological grounding of MECH-104 (LC-NE volatility interrupt): Yu & Dayan 2005 expected/unexpected uncertainty distinction, Aston-Jones & Cohen adaptive gain theory, Sara norepinephrine and memory consolidation, Bouret & Sara network reset hypothesis.
key_sources: Yu & Dayan (2005) - Uncertainty, neuromodulation and attention (expected vs unexpected uncertainty)
Aston-Jones & Cohen (2005) - An integrative theory of locus coeruleus-norepinephrine function: adaptive gain and optimal performance
Sara (2009) - The locus coeruleus and noradrenergic modulation of cognition
Bouret & Sara (2005) - Network reset: a simplified overarching theory of locus coeruleus noradrenaline function
items.executed_note: All four key sources now grounded in targeted_review_connectome_mech_104: Yu & Dayan 2005 (yudayan2005), Aston-Jones & Cohen 2005 (astonjones2005), Bouret & Sara 2005 (bouret2005), Sara 2009 (sara2009, added this session); Sara & Bouret 2012 orienting/reorienting (sarabouret2012) supplements as bonus source.

items.objective: Ground MECH-104 in computational uncertainty and change-point detection: Nassar surprise-gated learning, Behrens volatility inference, Mathys hierarchical Gaussian filter, Adams & MacKay Bayesian change-point.
key_sources: Nassar et al. (2010) - An approximately Bayesian delta-rule model explains the dynamics of belief updating in a changing environment
Behrens et al. (2007) - Learning the value of information in an uncertain world
Mathys et al. (2011) - A Bayesian foundation for individual learning under uncertainty (HGF)
Adams & MacKay (2007) - Bayesian online changepoint detection

items.objective: V3 core stack integration test: verify SD-008 + SD-007 + SD-003 compose correctly in a single pipeline run now that all three are validated individually. Test that alpha_world=0.9 + reafference correction (z_world_raw_prev input) + counterfactual attribution pipeline together produce expected event selectivity, harm calibration, and attribution accuracy.
items.design: Single full pipeline run with all three components active (alpha=0.9, SD-007 reafference enabled, SD-003 attribution pipeline). PASS: event_selectivity_margin > 0.05 (SD-008), world_forward_r2 > 0.8 (SD-007), attribution_gap > 0.02 (SD-003). This is an engineering integration check, not a new scientific claim.
items.notes: EXQ-023 originally attempted this combined test but failed because SD-007 input was wrong (z_self instead of z_world_raw_prev). All three components are now independently validated. Lower priority than ARC-007, ARC-029, MECH-104 pending experiments.

items.objective: Validate that E1 LSTM goal-conditioning (MECH-128) actually shapes E1's predictive distribution. PASS: E1 prediction error is significantly lower for goal-relevant state transitions than goal-irrelevant ones when z_goal_latent is provided vs withheld (interaction term), demonstrating that goal context modulates prediction rather than being a dormant input.
items.design: Condition A: E1 receives [x_t, z_goal_latent] (goal-conditioned). Condition B: E1 receives [x_t, zero_goal] (goal-ablated). Both conditions trained on the same goal-directed task. PASS criterion: goal-relevant prediction error A < B by margin > 0.05; goal-irrelevant prediction error A ~= B (goal conditioning should not degrade irrelevant predictions). Tests MECH-128 specifically -- not a duplicate of MECH-112 (z_goal existence) since it tests downstream effect on E1, not E3.
items.notes: BLOCKED on EXQ-074b completion (MECH-112/117 wanting/liking validation, v3_exq_074b_mech112_117_wanting_liking.py). Do not queue until EXQ-074b PASS confirmed -- experiment requires a functioning z_goal_latent to condition on. (Note: EXQ-091 was the V3 queue slot for EXQ-085d, a MECH-071 goal-persist test -- unrelated to MECH-112.)

items.objective: Reconfirm the post-SD-011 SD-003 attribution result in its current form: can the trained z_harm_s counterfactual attribution path cleanly beat an explicit ablation or scramble control across matched seeds?
items.design: Condition A: current post-SD-011 attribution pathway on z_harm_s (same architectural path as the successful 166e-style result). Condition B: explicit ablation or scramble control that preserves training budget but destroys the attribution mechanism. Use at least 3 matched seeds and one simple task variant. PASS: trained condition beats control on pre-registered attribution gap / harm-delta discrimination metrics with consistent sign across seeds and preserves escalation ordering. This is the first-paper attribution anchor and should be narratively simple.
items.notes: Run first. This is the clean paper-1 anchor for responsibility-like attribution in the current V3 substrate.

items.objective: Test whether the dual nociceptive stream substrate is stable enough to be treated as platform rather than rescue work.
items.design: Condition A: current dual-stream SD-011 stack (z_harm_s + z_harm_a active). Condition B: fused or stream-ablated control that collapses the distinction. Use matched seeds and one task variant that changes hazard structure without changing the core story. PASS: the dual-stream condition preserves the expected sensory-vs-affective dissociation and beats control on the pre-registered substrate metrics in the same direction across seeds and variant. This is a stability/replication pack, not a new theory search.
items.notes: This is the minimum substrate-stability check needed before treating Claim 1 as platform instead of ongoing rescue work.

items.objective: Resolve the representation-vs-selection ambiguity by injecting a handcrafted resource indicator into the goal path: if the selector gets an explicit goal cue, can it exploit it behaviorally?
items.design: Condition A: benefit path receives a handcrafted or oracle-grade resource indicator / goal cue that is invariant to spatial respawn. Condition B: matched sham or zero-goal control. Keep the rest of the stack unchanged. PASS: Condition A yields a clear navigation / resource-acquisition lift over control without requiring a new learned encoder. Interpretation: PASS -> selection path is usable and learned representation is the main remaining gap. FAIL -> action selection / benefit integration is still broken even with a good signal.
items.notes: This diagnostic should be run before more elaborate goal-encoder work if the current goal stack remains ambiguous.

items.objective: Show genuine goal-directed behavior with the current best learned goal signal: does GOAL_PRESENT beat GOAL_ABSENT under matched conditions?
items.design: Condition A: GOAL_PRESENT with the current best learned goal pathway active. Condition B: GOAL_ABSENT or goal-ablated matched control. Use matched seeds and pre-register a behavioral lift criterion based on resource acquisition / navigation success, while also checking that harm does not regress catastrophically. PASS: GOAL_PRESENT produces a reproducible positive behavioral lift over control. If this fails while EXP-0091 passes, the learned representation remains the bottleneck; if both fail, the selector/integration path remains the bottleneck.
items.notes: This is the decisive behavioral test for whether positive attractors are real in the current V3 stack.

items.objective: Demonstrate the selector payoff directly: does a shared harm-plus-goal selector outperform a one-sided selector in a hazard-bearing goal task?
items.design: Condition A: COMBINED selector with harm and goal evaluation active on the same candidate trajectories. Condition B: HARM_ONLY selector with goal pressure removed but identical trajectory generation and evaluation budget. Use a goal-bearing environment with non-trivial hazard pressure. PASS: COMBINED improves goal achievement or resource acquisition over HARM_ONLY without paying an unacceptable harm penalty. This is the cleanest behavioral test that approach and avoidance are adjudicated in one selector rather than bolted together externally.
items.notes: Paper-1 selector payoff test. If needed later, compare COMBINED vs GOAL_ONLY as a follow-up rather than broadening this first pair.

items.objective: Ground MECH-128 (E1 goal-context conditioning) in predictive coding and top-down goal modulation literature: Rao & Ballard 1999 (predictive coding, top-down context), Clark 2013 (predictive brains with goal-directed top-down), Friston 2010 (free-energy, goal context in generative models), Botvinick & Plaut 2004 (goal-conditioned recurrent networks for action sequences).
key_sources: Rao & Ballard (1999) - Predictive coding in the visual cortex: a functional interpretation and its application to development (Nature Neuroscience)
Clark (2013) - Whatever next? Predictive brains, situated agents, and the future of cognitive science (Behavioral and Brain Sciences)
Friston (2010) - The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory? (Nature Reviews Neuroscience)
Botvinick & Plaut (2004) - Doing without schema hierarchies: a recurrent connectionist approach to normal and impaired routine sequential action (Psychological Review)

items.objective: Ground MECH-129 (relational harm: harm-to-agent vs harm-to-agency) in philosophy of harm and AI alignment literature. Hart & Honore on causal obstruction, Thomson on trolley problem harm distinctions, Shue on agency as a protectable interest, Pearlman on goal obstruction as instrumental harm, Hadfield-Menell on other-agent goal inference.
key_sources: Hart & Honore (1959/1985) - Causation in the Law (Clarendon Press) -- causal obstruction of agency as harm
Thomson (1985) - The trolley problem (Yale Law Journal) -- harm-to-agency distinctions in applied ethics
Shue (1980) - Basic Rights: Subsistence, Affluence, and U.S. Foreign Policy (Princeton UP) -- agency as a protectable interest
Pearlman, Uesato & Leike (2019) - Risks from learned optimization in advanced machine learning systems (Alignment Forum) -- goal obstruction as instrumental harm
Hadfield-Menell, Milli, Abbeel, Russell & Dragan (2016) - Cooperative inverse reinforcement learning (NeurIPS) -- other-agent goal inference as prerequisite for harm-to-agency detection

items.objective: Ground MECH-130 (curiosity typing: world-state novelty vs agent-policy novelty) in intrinsic motivation and MARL exploration literature. Schmidhuber compression-based novelty, Oudeyer & Kaplan typology, Pathak ICM, Houthooft VIME, Baker multiagent autocurricula. Key note: absence of world vs agent novelty distinction in the literature may itself be a contribution.
key_sources: Schmidhuber (1991/2010) - Formal theory of creativity and curiosity and beauty (IEEE Transactions on Autonomous Mental Development) -- compression progress as novelty signal
Oudeyer & Kaplan (2007) - What is intrinsic motivation? A typology of computational approaches (Frontiers in Neurorobotics) -- systematic taxonomy of curiosity signals
Pathak, Agrawal, Efros & Darrell (2017) - Curiosity-driven exploration by self-supervised prediction (ICML) -- ICM: prediction error as novelty signal
Houthooft, Chen, Duan, Schulman, De Turck & Abbeel (2016) - VIME: variational information maximizing exploration (NeurIPS) -- information-theoretic novelty
Baker, Kanitscheider, Martic, Wu, Powell, McGrew & Mordatch (2019) - Emergent tool use from multi-agent autocurricula (ICLR) -- novelty in multi-agent play; agent behavior as perpetual novelty source
items.notes: Survey literature for any existing treatment of world-novelty vs agent-policy-novelty distinction. If absent, this gap is itself a potential contribution -- document in lit review.

items.objective: Replace hardcoded commitment_threshold=0.40 with k*warmup_baseline_variance (k=2) per EXQ-018b findings. Requires: (1) measuring baseline_variance during warmup episodes, (2) setting commitment_threshold = 2 * baseline_variance after warmup completes. Converts ARC-016 from manually-calibrated to self-calibrating as operating regime changes.
items.notes: Small implementation: ~3 lines in agent.py. Wait until more V3 experiments have run to establish reliable baseline_variance estimates before implementing.

items.objective: Characterize the hazard/resource ratio threshold above which the depression attractor forms (behavioral flattening: HABIT==PLANNED). EXQ-237a established the attractor exists at LONG_HORIZON parameters; this sweep identifies boundary conditions.
items.design: 3x3 grid sweep of hazard_rate (low/medium/high) x resource_rate (low/medium/high). For each cell, 3 seeds in HABIT and PLANNED mode. Measure HABIT/PLANNED behavioral equivalence score (|HABIT_harm_rate - PLANNED_harm_rate|). PASS: identify threshold ratio above which gap < 0.02 in majority (>=2/3) of seeds; produce ratio-to-gap curve showing transition.
items.notes: Immediately testable -- no new architecture needed, parameter extension of EXQ-237a. Key deliverable is the threshold estimate, not just PASS/FAIL.

items.objective: Systematically replicate and characterize the computational depression attractor from EXQ-237a seed=42. Establish that z_harm_a elevation + z_goal failure -> HABIT/PLANNED flattening is robust across seeds.
items.design: Multi-seed (5 seeds) replication of LONG_HORIZON condition from EXQ-237a (high hazard/resource ratio). Measure per seed: z_harm_a_mean, z_goal_norm, HABIT_harm_rate, PLANNED_harm_rate, behavioral_equivalence_score. PASS: >=4/5 seeds show z_goal_norm < 0.1 AND |HABIT_harm_rate - PLANNED_harm_rate| < 0.02 (modes indistinguishable).
items.notes: EXQ-237a seed=42 is preliminary evidence. This experiment provides the systematic replication needed to register INV-053 as active. Run before MECH-186/187/188 experiments.

items.objective: Test the phase-transition recovery prediction: once the depression attractor is established (LONG_HORIZON), switching to LOW_HARM environment produces non-linear delayed recovery, not immediate improvement.
items.design: Phase 1: train 3 seeds in LONG_HORIZON until depression attractor established (z_goal_norm < 0.1 for 50+ episodes). Phase 2: switch to LOW_HARM environment, continue training 300 additional episodes. Measure recovery trajectory: z_goal_norm and HABIT/PLANNED gap over phase 2 episodes. PASS: recovery trajectory shows latency >50 phase-2 episodes before z_goal_norm crosses 0.3 threshold (demonstrating delayed/phase-transition recovery, not immediate). FAIL: z_goal_norm recovers within 10 episodes (graded not phase-transition).
items.notes: INV-054 maintenance loop predicts that the depressive state is self-maintaining: z_goal absent -> no terrain exploration -> terrain stays collapsed. Recovery requires accumulated terrain re-exposure to cross a threshold. This is a unique prediction distinguishing INV-054 from simple reward-signal insufficiency accounts. V3-EXQ-435 supersedes V3-EXQ-278 with sustained-crossing recovery criterion (10 consecutive eps above 0.3, avg over last 30 steps) to distinguish attractor escape from single-episode bounces.

items.objective: Test whether artificially maintaining a minimum VALENCE_WANTING floor (simulating serotonergic benefit terrain maintenance) prevents depression attractor formation in LONG_HORIZON condition.
items.design: 2-condition x 3-seed matched design: (A) LONG_HORIZON/no_floor (baseline, depression expected), (B) LONG_HORIZON/floor_maintained (clip VALENCE_WANTING minimum=0.05 throughout training). Measure: z_goal_norm, |HABIT_harm_rate - PLANNED_harm_rate|. PASS: condition B shows z_goal_norm >= 0.15 AND HABIT/PLANNED gap >= 0.05 vs condition A (floor prevents depression attractor).
items.notes: Requires small implementation: floor clamp on VALENCE_WANTING in agent. Run EXP-0096 first to confirm depression attractor is robust before testing rescue mechanisms. Maps to environmental intervention and activity scheduling in therapy (externally supplied terrain restoration).

items.objective: Test bidirectional gain effects on z_goal seeding: reduced gain replicates depression in SIMPLE/LOW_HARM condition; elevated gain rescues LONG_HORIZON from depression attractor. Bidirectional prediction is a strong discriminating test.
items.design: 3-condition x 3-seed: (A) SIMPLE/normal_gain (baseline), (B) SIMPLE/reduced_gain (multiply z_goal seeding gain by 0.2), (C) LONG_HORIZON/elevated_gain (multiply gain by 5). Measure: z_goal_norm, HABIT/PLANNED gap. PASS: condition B shows z_goal_norm < 0.1 in SIMPLE (reduced gain induces depression in easy environment); condition C shows partial z_goal_norm rescue >= 0.10 in LONG_HORIZON (elevated gain partially restores seeding despite terrain collapse).
items.notes: Maps to SSRI mechanism: not mood elevation per se, but recalibration of the approach system responsiveness to terrain signals. Bidirectional prediction (gain reduction causes depression; gain elevation rescues) is what distinguishes MECH-187 from MECH-186 (terrain maintenance). Requires gain parameter in z_goal seeding module.

items.objective: Test whether injecting a sustained z_goal signal (simulating PFC top-down goal persistence) prevents behavioral flattening during LONG_HORIZON even when benefit terrain has collapsed.
items.design: 2-condition x 3-seed: (A) LONG_HORIZON/no_injection (baseline), (B) LONG_HORIZON/z_goal_injected (add constant z_goal_seed=0.3 to action selection phase, bypassing terrain-based seeding). Measure: HABIT/PLANNED behavioral gap, harm_rate. PASS: injection maintains HABIT/PLANNED gap >= 0.05 in LONG_HORIZON (terrain collapsed but external goal scaffolding preserves goal-directed behavior).
items.notes: Maps to behavioural activation therapy: injecting goal structure that the terrain can no longer supply spontaneously. Key test: is MECH-188 sufficient in isolation? INV-052 (EXP-0101) tests whether any single mechanism is sufficient. MECH-188 is a top-down override, distinct from terrain manipulation (MECH-186) and gain regulation (MECH-187).

items.objective: Test whether any single serotonergic rescue mechanism (MECH-186, 187, or 188 in isolation) fully restores LONG_HORIZON performance to SIMPLE baseline, or whether full recovery requires all three. Confirms INV-052: tonic regulatory system is necessary across all three pipeline stages.
items.design: 5-condition x 3-seed: (A) SIMPLE/no_interventions (healthy baseline), (B) LONG_HORIZON/no_interventions (depression baseline), (C) LONG_HORIZON/terrain_floor_only (MECH-186), (D) LONG_HORIZON/elevated_gain_only (MECH-187), (E) LONG_HORIZON/goal_injection_only (MECH-188). Compare z_goal_norm and HABIT/PLANNED gap for each condition vs healthy baseline. PASS: no single mechanism (C, D, or E) recovers to >= 80% of SIMPLE baseline on both metrics; partial rescue by each individually confirms distinct stages but joint necessity.
items.notes: Depends on EXP-0098, EXP-0099, EXP-0100 completing first for parameter calibration of each mechanism. Should run after those three complete. A full combined-mechanisms condition (all three together) can optionally be added as condition F to test whether all three together do restore baseline.

items.objective: Ground INV-053 (computational depression attractor) in computational psychiatry, distinguishing motivational terrain collapse from aberrant reward learning. Huys/Dayan/Adams lineage focuses on incorrect Q-values and precision weighting; INV-053 is about the substrate (benefit terrain, z_goal seeding) degrading. Establish this as a distinct failure mode.
key_sources: Huys, Daw & Dayan (2021) - Depression: A decision-theoretic analysis (Annual Review of Neuroscience) -- reward learning in depression, pruning, and effort
Adams, Huys & Roiser (2016) - Computational psychiatry: towards a mathematically informed understanding of mental illness (Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry)
Dayan & Huys (2008) - Serotonin, inhibition, and negative mood (PLOS Computational Biology) -- 5-HT and reward modulation
Eshel & Roiser (2010) - Reward and punishment processing in depression (Biological Psychiatry) -- anhedonia as reward sensitivity deficit
Treadway & Zald (2011) - Reconsidering anhedonia in depression: lessons from translational neuroscience (Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews) -- wanting vs liking in clinical anhedonia
items.notes: Key framing contribution: REE locates depression at the terrain/seeding layer, not the value-learning layer. This is architecturally distinct from existing computational psychiatry models and should be documented as such in the paper. Executed 2026-04-18: 4 of 5 proposed sources already covered in targeted_review_inv_053 (Adams 2016, Dayan 2008, Eshel 2010, Treadway 2010); added Huys/Daw/Dayan 2015 decision-theoretic review as 5th entry (the proposal's '2021' date was incorrect -- the Annual Review paper is 2015, PMID 25705929).

items.objective: Ground MECH-187 (incentive salience gain regulation by serotonin) in 5-HT neuromodulation and incentive salience literature. Serotonin modulates dopaminergic incentive salience attribution; MECH-187 formalizes this as gain on z_goal seeding from benefit terrain signals.
key_sources: Berridge & Robinson (1998) - What is the role of dopamine in reward: hedonic impact, reward learning, or incentive salience? (Brain Research Reviews) -- canonical wanting/liking dissociation
Dayan & Huys (2009) - Serotonin in affective control (Annual Review of Neuroscience) -- 5-HT as opponent modulator of dopaminergic reward
Cools, Roberts & Robbins (2008) - Serotoninergic regulation of emotional and behavioural control processes (Trends in Cognitive Sciences) -- 5-HT and behavioral control
Robinson & Berridge (2003) - Addiction and incentive-sensitization theory (Addiction) -- incentive salience, gain modulation, and sensitization
Lim et al. (2020) or Fonseca et al. (2015) - Serotonin modulation of dopamine-dependent reward and incentive salience (PFC/VTA/VLS circuit)

items.objective: Ground INV-054 (depressive maintenance loop as self-sustaining attractor; recovery as phase transition) in clinical depression phenomenology and treatment response literature. Phase-transition prediction (non-linear, delayed recovery) is a unique testable consequence of the INV-054 maintenance loop.
key_sources: Nesse (2000) - Is depression an adaptation? (Archives of General Psychiatry) -- ecological/functional framing of depression maintenance
Petersen et al. (2014) or Gotlib & Hammen (2008) -- depression maintenance and rumination as self-sustaining loop
Kupfer (1991) - Long-term treatment of depression (Journal of Clinical Psychiatry) -- antidepressant response latency as empirical constraint
Trivedi et al. (STAR*D, 2006) - Evaluation of outcomes with citalopram for depression (American Journal of Psychiatry) -- treatment response as non-linear process with substantial latency
Mathew, Manji & Charney (2008) - Novel drugs and therapeutic targets for severe mood disorders (Neuropsychopharmacology) -- phase-transition framing of antidepressant mechanism
items.notes: The key empirical constraint is treatment response latency (2-4 weeks for SSRIs). If recovery were graded, immediate partial response would be expected. Delayed non-linear response supports attractor/phase-transition account. This grounding is important for the paper's psychiatric section credibility.

items.objective: Re-run EXQ-228a theta bypass behavioral test after EXQ-247 resolves z_goal seeding. Protocol unchanged -- the experiment design is sound, only the substrate precondition (z_goal_norm >= 0.05) was unmet.
items.design: Gate: EXQ-247 must PASS first (z_goal_norm >= 0.1). Then re-queue EXQ-228a protocol (THETA_ACTIVE vs THETA_ZEROED, 3 seeds, resource_rate lift >= 0.05). No redesign needed.
items.notes: Design-forward analysis DR-1: z_goal seeding requires SD-012 resolution. See governance_meta_illusory_conflict_resolution.md and design_forward_analysis_2026-04-06.md.

items.objective: Test Go/NoGo sub-channel symmetry with real z_goal. Requires both z_goal_norm >= 0.1 AND z_goal alignment with resource proximity (align_corr > 0.2). Two gates.
items.design: Gate 1: EXQ-247 PASS. Gate 2: z_goal alignment test (re-run EXQ-235 protocol). If both pass, new Go/NoGo experiment with approach sub-channel activated by real z_goal. 2x2: Go_active vs Go_ablated x NoGo_active vs NoGo_ablated.
items.supersedes: EXQ-235 (non-contributory: z_goal alignment failure), EXQ-086 (measurement bug)
items.notes: Design-forward analysis DR-2: z_goal norm and alignment are independent failure modes. SD-015 (resource indicator encoding) is a separate prerequisite.

items.objective: Redesigned dual-system discrimination test. Must include: (a) repeated identical environments where monostrategy IS the correct behavior (habit detection phase), then (b) novel environments where planned pathway should add value over habit.
items.design: Phase 1 (habit training): 50 episodes in a single fixed environment. Measure monostrategy convergence rate as habit proxy. Phase 2 (novelty test): switch to novel environment. Compare HABIT_ONLY vs HABIT+PLANNED conditions. PASS: planned adds lift in novel environment, habit dominates in familiar. Requires z_goal_norm >= 0.1.
items.supersedes: EXQ-237a (non-contributory: z_goal substrate + task paradigm failure)
items.notes: Design-forward analysis DR-3: even the habit condition showed zero lift in EXQ-237a. Task design must make habit formation observable before testing planned-system value.

items.objective: Redesigned replay diversity test. Must pair replay mechanism with explicit exploration to provide diverse source material. Test asks: does balanced replay MAINTAIN diversity that exploration created?
items.design: Phase 1 (exploration): warmup with epsilon=0.5 random exploration, generating diverse behavioral trajectories. Phase 2 (consolidation + test): FORWARD_REPLAY vs BALANCED_REPLAY vs NO_REPLAY. PASS: BALANCED maintains action_entropy from Phase 1 better than FORWARD (which should collapse to monostrategy). 5 seeds.
items.supersedes: EXQ-244 (weakens: test design flaw -- asked replay to CREATE diversity rather than MAINTAIN it)
items.notes: Design-forward analysis DR-9: replay cannot increase entropy without alternative strategies as source material. Current EXQ-244 asked replay to diversify from monostrategy -- impossible by definition. MECH-165 substrate implemented 2026-04-09; EXQ-244a queued.

items.objective: Redesigned sleep phase ablation with context-conditioned action selection. EXQ-242 showed slot differentiation works (4/5 seeds) but agent doesn't use context knowledge. Add context->action pathway.
items.design: Add context-conditioned harm_eval threshold: harm_threshold = f(active_context_slot). In DANGEROUS context, lower threshold (more cautious). In SAFE context, higher threshold (more exploratory). Re-run EXQ-242 conditions (WAKING_ONLY, SWS_ONLY, SWS_THEN_REM). PASS: C1 (slot diff) AND C2 (harm rate benefit from context-conditioning). 5 seeds.
items.supersedes: EXQ-242 (diagnostic: slot differentiation works but no behavioral path)
items.notes: Design-forward analysis DR-6: missing link is between context_memory readout and action selection. The simplest proxy is context-conditioned harm_eval threshold.

items.objective: First V3 test of SD-029 (single-pass z_harm_s comparator). Replace mean-cf-gap with event-conditioned residual analysis on approach-to-harm events; include event-density controls (guaranteed minimum agent-caused-hazard events per seed) to fix the EXQ-431 sample-starvation failure mode.
items.design: Train E2_harm_s with SD-013 interventional contrast (counterfactual perturbation during training). Evaluate residual_harm = z_harm_s_observed - E2_harm_s(z_harm_s_{t-1}, a_actual) per step. Tag steps by event type (approach-to-harm, self-caused-hazard, externally-caused-hazard, baseline). Compute residual magnitude per event class, expect partial attenuation on self-caused relative to externally-caused (Shergill ~50% pattern). PASS: C1 forward_r2 >= 0.9; C2 self-vs-external attenuation ratio between 0.3-0.7 (partial, not binary); C3 SNR > 3 on approach-to-harm events. Event-density control: reject seeds with <20 agent-caused-hazard events; resample until density target met. 4 seeds.
items.notes: Successor-to-SD-003 test. Single-pass comparator, no counterfactual branch. Script to be written as v3_exq_433_sd029_eventcond_comparator.py; not yet queued -- pending script implementation.

items.objective: Test MECH-257 dual-function claim: does a single E2_harm_s substrate support both retrospective comparator (SD-029 attribution) and prospective rollout-scoring (evaluator) effectively, or does joint training degrade one mode relative to mode-split baselines?
items.design: Three-arm ablation. Arm A: E2_harm_s trained comparator-only (post-action residual loss). Arm B: trained evaluator-only (rollout value loss). Arm C: trained jointly with mode-label gating input. Measure per-arm: comparator SNR on approach-to-harm events; evaluator accuracy (predicted vs realised trajectory value) at horizon k=5,10. PASS (MECH-257 supported): Arm C >= 85% of Arm A's comparator SNR AND >= 85% of Arm B's evaluator accuracy. FAIL: Arm C significantly degrades one or both modes -> separate substrates required. 4 seeds.
items.notes: Follow-up to EXP-0146. Queue only after SD-029 comparator baseline establishes a reproducible signal.

prerequisites: SD-032a implemented (salience-network coordinator emitting operating_mode vector)
MECH-261 implemented (at least E3 policy-write gate, PFC-analog write gate, and cortical-sensory buffer write gate keyed to mode vector)
items.objective: Falsify (or support) the MECH-261 claim that a shared operating-mode vector from SD-032a is load-bearing for forward-propagation of internal_replay content into subsequent external_task action selection. Core hypothesis: lesioning the coordinator abolishes the post-quiescence action-selection bias; local per-substrate gating alone does not reproduce it.
items.design: Three-arm ablation on a fixed foraging task with scheduled micro-quiescence windows (MECH-092). (A) Intact: SD-032a emits the full operating_mode vector; gates read the vector normally. (B) Coordinator ablated: SD-032a output clamped to a constant external_task one-hot; per-substrate gates retain their local mode-dependent logic but receive a fixed input. (C) No-mode-gating null: gates replaced with permanently-open local heuristics (no dependence on operating_mode). Across arms measure: (i) post-quiescence trajectory-selection bias toward recently replayed trajectories (Tambini-Davachi-analog: cosine similarity between replay content and next-trajectory embedding); (ii) cross-mode write bleed during external_task commitment (writes to viability map originating from replay content); (iii) downstream policy change rate per unit replay event. PASS (MECH-261 supported): Arm A > Arm B ~= Arm C on metric (i); Arm A shows segregated write bleed while B and C do not. FAIL-coordinator (MECH-261 over-specified for coordinator role): Arm B reproduces Arm A on metric (i) -- gating is local. FAIL-gate (MECH-261 over-specified entirely): Arm C reproduces Arm A -- mode gating unnecessary. 5 seeds per arm; success threshold = pairwise Cohen's d >= 0.8 on metric (i) between distinguishing arms.
items.notes: Causal test of the salience-network-as-coordinator overlay flagged in targeted_review_systems_consolidation_waking_propagation/synthesis.md. Rodent replay literature does not directly validate the coordinator role; this is a REE-specific prediction. Cannot run until V3 SD-032 cluster is at least minimally implemented. Metric (i) maps onto Tambini & Davachi 2019 forward-cognitive-bias finding; metric (ii) onto Rothschild et al 2017 cortical-hippocampal-cortical loop; metric (iii) onto Peyrache et al 2009 PFC-replay-drives-rule-update.

prerequisites: SD-032a implemented (salience-network coordinator emitting operating_mode vector over {external_task, internal_planning, internal_replay, offline_consolidation})
SD-033a implemented (lateral-PFC-analog rule/goal substrate, primary MECH-261 write target under external_task / internal_planning)
SD-033b implemented (OFC-analog specific-outcome prediction substrate; E2 rollout address)
MECH-261 implemented as per-subdivision write-gate family (not just an aggregate coordinator overlay): each SD-033 subdivision exposes a write-gate keyed to the operating_mode vector
MECH-092 active (micro-quiescence SWR-analog replay generator) so that internal_replay mode is exercised distinctly from external_task
items.objective: Test whether MECH-261's per-subdivision write-landing profile is correctly mode-routed -- distinct from the coordinator-level test in EXP-0148. EXP-0148 asks whether the coordinator overlay produces the right aggregate forward-propagation bias; EXP-0149 asks whether writes actually land in the correct PFC subdivision (SD-033a vs SD-033b) under each mode. A coordinator could pass EXP-0148 by producing the right aggregate bias while writes are mis-routed internally between subdivisions; only a per-subdivision lesion design can dissociate that.
items.design: Four-arm per-subdivision write-gate manipulation on a fixed task that includes scheduled internal_replay windows (MECH-092) and explicit internal_planning rollouts (E2) in addition to external_task action selection. Arm A (intact): all SD-033 write-gates obey their MECH-261 mode profile normally. Arm B (SD-033a write-gate forced open during internal_replay): the lateral-PFC-analog rule substrate becomes writeable from replay content during quiescence -- replay can overwrite the rule substrate. Predicted signature: rule drift / forgetting after replay events. Arm C (SD-033b write-gate forced closed during internal_planning): the OFC-analog outcome-prediction substrate cannot accept writes during E2 rollouts -- planning cannot deposit speculative outcome predictions. Predicted signature: degraded counterfactual rollout quality (E2 outcome predictions lose calibration against held-out rollout-realisation pairs). Arm D (null: no mode gating at all): all SD-033 substrates are write-open in every mode. Predicted signature: cross-mode contamination in BOTH the rule substrate (rule drift) AND the outcome substrate (replay content bleeding into external-task outcome predictions). Per arm, measure: (i) rule-representation drift across replay events -- cosine distance of SD-033a activity for the same rule cue pre vs post a replay window; (ii) counterfactual rollout quality -- E2 / SD-033b prediction accuracy against held-out (state, action) -> outcome pairs after planning episodes; (iii) cross-mode content leakage -- decoder probe testing whether internal_replay content (replayed trajectory embeddings) is detectable in SD-033b representations during external_task commitment. Success criterion (MECH-261 per-subdivision profile supported): per-arm signatures dissociate in the predicted direction with pairwise Cohen's d >= 0.8 between Arm A and each manipulated arm on at least 5 seeds, AND Arm A is statistically distinguishable from Arm D on both rule drift and rollout quality. FAIL-route-A (rule substrate not protected by mode gating): Arm B == Arm A on rule drift -- forced-open SD-033a write-gate produces no rule drift, suggesting the mode profile is not load-bearing for the rule substrate specifically. FAIL-route-B (planning substrate write-gate redundant): Arm C == Arm A on rollout quality -- closing SD-033b during planning does not degrade rollouts, suggesting writes were not landing there during planning anyway. FAIL-cluster (mode gating per se unnecessary): Arm D == Arm A on all three metrics -- even fully open gates produce no contamination, mode-conditioned write gating is over-specified.
items.notes: Sibling experiment to EXP-0148, NOT a replacement. EXP-0148 tests the coordinator overlay (does SD-032a-clamping abolish aggregate forward-propagation bias?); EXP-0149 tests per-subdivision write-landing under each mode (do writes actually land in the correct SD-033 subdivision?). Both tests are needed to fully characterise MECH-261: EXP-0148 establishes that the coordinator is load-bearing at all; EXP-0149 establishes that the per-subdivision routing inside the MECH-261 family is functionally specific (not just one coarse on/off gate dressed up as a vector). A coordinator could pass EXP-0148 while failing EXP-0149 by producing the right aggregate bias with mis-routed internal writes; that pattern would force a refinement of MECH-261's specification. Cannot run until SD-032a + SD-033a + SD-033b + MECH-261 + MECH-092 are all implemented in V3 -- proposal-only at registration time. Do not queue in ree-v3/experiment_queue.json until those substrates exist.
